procedure.title: Collect
procedure.description: Teams of 3–5 deploy micro:bit sensors at locations around the school. Record temperature, light, and humidity at 5-minute intervals. Note direct observations — clouds, wind, shade patterns. Build a shared data table.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams input data into the AI analysis tool. AI generates predictions and pattern descriptions. Students compare: Does the AI match what we measured? Where does it agree? Where does it diverge? Document at least three points of agreement and three of disagreement.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Each team applies Check the Machine. Teams present: Where was AI accurate? Where did it miss? What did human observation catch that AI did not? Class develops shared guidelines for trusting AI with environmental data.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we collected?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses and instruments?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Given what we found, should we trust, adjust, or override the AI?

assessment.description: One-page Trust Report: where AI was accurate, where it failed, evidence-based recommendation.
assessment.criteria: Quality of evidence and reasoning, not whether student agreed with AI.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The Check the Machine protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol Check the Machine. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Lakota Local SD)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Smart Choices: How AI Helps (and Misleads) Decision-Making" (grades 6-8, math/science). Students use micro:bit to collect data, then compare their own analysis with AI-generated conclusions. Embedded AI literacy: students identify where AI adds value and where it introduces risk. Designed to produce durable skills and materials — aligned with Lakota's culture of measured investment.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, AI conclusions look like answers worth acting on. After: students compare their own analysis with the AI output and identify where the AI adds value versus where it introduces decision risk — exactly the discipline a fiscally engaged community demands.

unplugged.title: The Budget Argument: Should We Trust AI With Public Decisions?
unplugged.overview: Teams receive printed data about a fictional school budget decision. They build an argument, then examine an AI-generated recommendation for the same data. Structured debate format. Designed for Lakota's fiscally engaged community.
content_objectives: Analyze printed data or scenarios and identify patterns, gaps, assumptions
Construct evidence-based arguments using only printed materials and group reasoning
Compare human judgment with algorithmic approaches
ai_literacy_objectives: Evaluate how AI systems are trained and what assumptions they carry
Identify the difference between a model's confidence and its accuracy
Apply Check the Machine using only printed evidence and group reasoning
materials: Printed data cards or scenario materials (PDF at landing page)
Check the Machine reference card (included in kit)

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3–5 receive printed materials. Review data, identify key patterns, note what is present and missing. Discuss: What would an AI see? What would it miss?

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Each team applies Check the Machine using only printed evidence. Answer: What data was available? What was missing? Would someone who knows Liberty Twp have made a different call? Record reasoning on chart paper.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Gallery walk: teams post analysis and visit others' work. Class discussion: When trust algorithmic recommendations? When should local knowledge override? Each student writes a one-paragraph position statement.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: One-paragraph position statement on when to trust AI vs. human judgment.
assessment.criteria: Specificity of evidence and quality of reasoning.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Check the Machine — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part protocol called Check the Machine: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Lakota Local SD)

Describe tradeoffs between public and private information
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Use collaborative tech to examine issues from multiple views
Compare historical/contemporary means of changing society

unplugged.district_framing: The Budget Argument: Should We Trust AI With Public Decisions? — Teams receive printed data about a fictional school budget decision. They build an argument, then examine an AI-generated recommendation for the same data. Structured debate format. Designed for Lakota's fiscally engaged community.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, an AI budget recommendation would close the debate. After: students build their own argument first, then examine the AI recommendation, and learn to defend their reasoning in a structured public-decision format.

45011-lkta.policyLandscape: Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model AI policy available; most districts will adopt or adapt; operationalization is the real work
45011-lkta.districtNeeds: Most Ohio districts will adopt the Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model policy or a close variant — the governance piece is straightforward. The harder, more important work is operationalizing it: what does teaching and learning actually look like when AI tools are available to students and educators? The model policy calls for 'AI literacy' and 'clearly articulated expectations for acceptable student uses,' but translating that language into classroom practice requires structured time for teachers to explore, build, and develop their own pedagogical approaches. That is the work Freyja Labs supports — grounded in the CRAFT framework (Contextualize, Reframe, Assemble, Fortify, Transfer), which gives educators a repeatable process for designing instruction that holds up as tools and contexts change.
45011-lkta.contextNarrative: Lakota is the largest district in Butler County and the suburban Cincinnati district most known for fiscal discipline and academic performance — a community that respects efficiency and rewards substance. AI literacy PD here needs to clear that bar: not a one-day exposure event, but a defensible investment teachers and administrators can point to as a result. Ohio's model AI policy will give every district the governance scaffolding; what makes the difference at the building level is whether teachers leave a workshop with materials and practices they actually use. CRAFT is designed for that — a process teachers internalize and continue using long after the workshop ends.

44308-aps.connectionPoint: One of the earliest AI literacy pilot districts in the U.S. (aiEDU partnership, 2020). Already has context — PD should build on what teachers know, not start from zero.
44308-aps.standardsFramework: Ohio Learning Standards (OLS). CS: Ohio CS Model Curriculum. Science: Ohio's Learning Standards for Science. Math: Ohio's Learning Standards for Mathematics. AI literacy objectives: align to Ohio AI in Education Model Policy framework.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Check the Machine — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

Note what the AI got right and wrong

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part protocol called Check the Machine: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Akron Public Schools)

standards_alignment.text: Reading Informational Text — Grade 7
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Describe tradeoffs between public and private information
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Use collaborative tech to examine issues from multiple views
Compare historical/contemporary means of changing society

unplugged.district_framing: AI Then vs. AI Now: What Changed and What Didn't — Since Akron started AI literacy work in 2020, students examine printed examples of AI outputs from 2020 vs. 2026. What improved? What problems persist? What does this tell us about trusting AI over time? Tailored to Akron's head start.
unplugged.distinction: Without the PD, comparing AI outputs across years would be a history lesson. After: students name exactly which problems AI fixed, which ones persist, and what that pattern says about which AI claims to trust over time.

44308-aps.policyLandscape: Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model AI policy available; most districts will adopt or adapt; operationalization is the real work
44308-aps.districtNeeds: Most Ohio districts will adopt the Ohio Department of Education and Workforce model policy or a close variant — the governance piece is straightforward. The harder, more important work is operationalizing it: what does teaching and learning actually look like when AI tools are available to students and educators? The model policy calls for 'AI literacy' and 'clearly articulated expectations for acceptable student uses,' but translating that language into classroom practice requires structured time for teachers to explore, build, and develop their own pedagogical approaches. That is the work Freyja Labs supports — grounded in the CRAFT framework (Contextualize, Reframe, Assemble, Fortify, Transfer), which gives educators a repeatable process for designing instruction that holds up as tools and contexts change.
44308-aps.contextNarrative: Akron Public Schools was one of the earliest AI literacy pilot districts in the U.S., with the aiEDU partnership going back to 2020. That history matters. PD here should not start from "what is AI?" — it should build on what teachers already know, deepen the practice they have begun, and connect it to Ohio's emerging policy frame. The CRAFT framework and Check the Machine protocol are designed to do that kind of layered work: not introductory exposure, but pedagogical depth for educators who are ready to design instruction at the next level of sophistication.

43604-tps.connectionPoint: Northwest Ohio manufacturing-to-tech economic transition. Logistics, healthcare, and technology sectors emerging. Computational thinking connects to regional workforce.

image.alt: Toledo waterfront along the Maumee River

43604-tps.standardsFramework: Ohio Learning Standards (OLS). CS: Ohio CS Model Curriculum. Science: Ohio's Learning Standards for Science. Math: Ohio's Learning Standards for Mathematics. AI literacy objectives: align to Ohio AI in Education Model Policy framework.

tech.title: Sensors and the Supply Chain: Data in a City That Moves
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Toledo Public Schools students in Toledo.
content_objectives: Collect real-time environmental data using micro:bit sensors deployed across campus
Analyze patterns in collected data using tables and graphs
Compare empirical measurements with model-generated predictions
ai_literacy_objectives: Evaluate AI-generated predictions against student-collected sensor data
Identify conditions under which AI predictions are reliable vs. misleading
Apply the Check the Machine protocol to decide when to trust, verify, or override AI
materials: AI analysis tool access (at landing page)

procedure.title: Collect
procedure.description: Teams of 3–5 deploy micro:bit sensors at locations around the school. Record temperature, light, and humidity at 5-minute intervals. Note direct observations — clouds, wind, shade patterns. Build a shared data table.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams input data into the AI analysis tool. AI generates predictions and pattern descriptions. Students compare: Does the AI match what we measured? Where does it agree? Where does it diverge? Document at least three points of agreement and three of disagreement.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Each team applies Check the Machine. Teams present: Where was AI accurate? Where did it miss? What did human observation catch that AI did not? Class develops shared guidelines for trusting AI with environmental data.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we collected?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses and instruments?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Given what we found, should we trust, adjust, or override the AI?

assessment.description: One-page Trust Report: where AI was accurate, where it failed, evidence-based recommendation.
assessment.criteria: Quality of evidence and reasoning, not whether student agreed with AI.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The Check the Machine protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The Check the Machine protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply Check the Machine to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol Check the Machine. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Toledo Public Schools)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Sensors and the Supply Chain: How Data Moves Through a City" (grades 7-9, science/CTE). Students use micro:bit sensors to simulate supply-chain monitoring, then use AI to analyze logistics data. Embedded AI literacy: students evaluate AI recommendations against real-world constraints. Tailored to Toledo's logistics and manufacturing economy.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, AI logistics analysis would be a workforce-prep topic. After: students build supply-chain monitoring with their own sensors and evaluate AI recommendations against real-world constraints — the gap between simulation and the working economy.

unplugged.badge: No Technology Required · Embedded AI Literacy
unplugged.title: The Supply Chain Puzzle: What Happens When the Data Is Wrong?
unplugged.overview: Teams receive printed logistics scenarios where supply-chain data contains errors. They trace how bad data cascades through an AI system and propose human checkpoints. Connected to Toledo's logistics economy.
content_objectives: Analyze printed data or scenarios and identify patterns, gaps, assumptions
Construct evidence-based arguments using only printed materials and group reasoning
Compare human judgment with algorithmic approaches
ai_literacy_objectives: Evaluate how AI systems are trained and what assumptions they carry
Identify the difference between a model's confidence and its accuracy
Apply Check the Machine using only printed evidence and group reasoning
materials: Printed data cards or scenario materials (PDF at landing page)
Check the Machine reference card (included in kit)

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3–5 receive printed materials. Review data, identify key patterns, note what is present and missing. Discuss: What would an AI see? What would it miss?

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Each team applies Check the Machine using only printed evidence. Answer: What data was available? What was missing? Would someone who knows Toledo have made a different call? Record reasoning on chart paper.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Gallery walk: teams post analysis and visit others' work. Class discussion: When trust algorithmic recommendations? When should local knowledge override? Each student writes a one-paragraph position statement.

Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The Check the Machine protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply Check the Machine to your divergences

Class builds the shared trust framework together

Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol Check the Machine. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Houston ISD)

standards_alignment.text: Apply scientific principles to design monitoring method for human impact
Analyze data from tests to determine best design solutions
Statistics & Probability — Grade 6
Model with mathematics — Math Practice
Collect data using computational tools; transform for reliability
Refine computational models based on generated data
Discuss bias and accessibility in technology design
Evaluate accuracy, credibility, relevance of information
Collect data and identify data sets; use tools to analyze data
Collect, transform, and analyze data using digital tools and explain how the data inform a decision
Use computational tools to collect, transform, analyze, and visualize data to answer a question
Use computational models, including simulations, to investigate phenomena and explain results
Use text evidence to support an appropriate response

tech.district_framing: Kit: micro:bit + sample lesson plan — "Weather, Data, and Trust: When Should We Believe What AI Tells Us?" (grades 6-8, science). Students collect weather data with micro:bit sensors, compare with AI-generated forecasts, and evaluate accuracy. Embedded AI literacy: Check the Machine protocol applied to AI weather predictions — when to trust, when to verify, when to override. Tailored to Houston's relationship with weather (hurricanes, flooding) — data literacy is personal here.
tech.distinction: Without the PD, an AI weather forecast lands as the forecast. After: students measure conditions themselves and learn that local data is the ground truth — the move that matters most when the forecast involves a Houston bayou.

unplugged.badge: No Technology Required · Embedded AI Literacy
unplugged.title: The Hurricane Forecast Challenge: Who Do You Trust With Your Family's Safety?
unplugged.overview: Teams examine printed historical hurricane forecast data (predicted vs. actual paths). They apply Check the Machine to determine when AI forecasts were reliable and when local knowledge — the bayous, the drainage patterns, the flat terrain — would have produced better decisions. Every Houston student who has watched a bayou rise has data no training set contains.
content_objectives: Analyze printed data or scenarios and identify patterns, gaps, assumptions
Construct evidence-based arguments using only printed materials and group reasoning
Compare human judgment with algorithmic approaches
ai_literacy_objectives: Evaluate how AI systems are trained and what assumptions they carry
Identify the difference between a model's confidence and its accuracy
Apply Check the Machine using only printed evidence and group reasoning
materials: Printed data cards or scenario materials (PDF at landing page)
Check the Machine reference card (included in kit)

procedure.title: Examine
procedure.description: Teams of 3–5 receive printed materials. Review data, identify key patterns, note what is present and missing. Discuss: What would an AI see? What would it miss?

procedure.title: Investigate
procedure.description: Each team applies Check the Machine using only printed evidence. Answer: What data was available? What was missing? Would someone who knows Houston have made a different call? Record reasoning on chart paper.

procedure.title: Argue
procedure.description: Gallery walk: teams post analysis and visit others' work. Class discussion: When trust algorithmic recommendations? When should local knowledge override? Each student writes a one-paragraph position statement.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did it reach its conclusion?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we know?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Should we trust, adjust, or override?

assessment.description: One-paragraph position statement on when to trust AI vs. human judgment.
assessment.criteria: Specificity of evidence and quality of reasoning.

extensions: Research a real-world AI decision affecting a community like yours
Design an unplugged activity for younger students
Use CxEdHub to create a tech-enhanced version

prior_knowledge: Read and discuss informational text in small groups
Cite evidence to support a claim — written or verbal
Familiarity with the difference between a prediction and a confirmed result

facilitation_notes.focus: Print the artifact packets in color so detail is preserved. Don't tell students which AI claims are "right" — let them notice divergence on their own. Their lived knowledge of the topic IS the comparison standard. Treat it that way explicitly.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that pick a "winning" artifact immediately. Slow them down — every artifact reflects the AI's best guess given its inputs. The question is not which is right but how anyone could have known in advance.

facilitation_notes.focus: Distinguish three error types: factual (X is asserted but isn't true), framing (the description emphasizes one thing while ignoring others), absence (something important is left out entirely). Most AI artifacts fail in framing and absence, not facts.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that only catch factual errors. Push deeper — what story is the AI telling? Whose perspective is implicit? What did it not have access to?

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the argument as advice to a real decision-maker who will act on it. Students must commit to a recommendation AND name specifically what would change their mind.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Hedging ("we can't really know"). True — but the decision still has to be made. Push students to commit to a recommendation AND explain what new information would flip it.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI is just like a calculator — if you give it the right numbers, you get the right answer."
misconceptions.response: Use a worked example where two students give the same prompt and get different outputs. AI is more like a human reader making a judgment call than a calculator computing a formula.

misconceptions.misconception: "If we can't see the math, we just have to trust it."
misconceptions.response: Pivot the protocol — "Check the reasoning" — to focus on what we CAN check: source, comparison to known facts, internal consistency. You don't need the math to evaluate a claim.

misconceptions.misconception: "AI hallucinations only happen with chatbots."
misconceptions.response: Show a printed AI example that contains a confident but factually wrong statement. Hallucinations are a property of how generative models work, not a chatbot quirk.

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

slide_cues.title: Real decisions, real evidence
talking_points: AI tools are showing up in decisions that matter
Your job today: evaluate whether the AI's reasoning holds up
No screens. Just the artifacts in front of you.

slide_cues.title: Check the Machine — without the machine
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
Same protocol whether you're looking at a sensor reading or a printed AI claim
Adapt to what's in front of you

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Examine
talking_points: Read the artifacts carefully
Note what the AI got right and wrong
Don't pick a winner yet

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Investigate
talking_points: WHY did the AI reach this conclusion?
What data did it have? What did it miss?
Was it the model, or what the model knew?

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Argue
talking_points: Make a defensible recommendation
"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"
Cite evidence from the packet

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: Three things to check before accepting any AI recommendation

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part protocol called Check the Machine: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Houston ISD)

77092-hisd.policyLandscape: CS = LOTE sub; TEA shortage area
77092-hisd.districtNeeds: Texas districts have autonomy in how they approach CS and AI, and the CS teacher-shortage designation signals that demand is outpacing the supply of dedicated specialists. The more sustainable path is building computational thinking capacity across content areas — helping existing teachers develop pedagogical approaches that integrate computing into their practice. As AI tools become more present in classrooms, teachers also need a structured way to help students engage with those tools critically. The CRAFT framework provides that structure, and the Check the Machine protocol gives students a repeatable verification practice they can apply across subjects and tools.
77092-hisd.contextNarrative: Houston ISD is the largest district in Texas and currently state-managed, with the Bluebonnet Learning curriculum initiative shaping the instructional landscape. The TEA's CS teacher-shortage designation tells the rest of the story: demand for computing instruction is outpacing the supply of dedicated specialists, and the sustainable path is helping existing teachers across content areas build computational thinking pedagogy they can use in their own classrooms. CRAFT provides the lesson design structure for that work, and the Check the Machine protocol gives Houston students a verification practice they can apply across subjects and tools — durable habits that travel with them past whatever curriculum or AI tool happens to be in use this year.

75231-disd.connectionPoint: Nationally recognized turnaround story. Supt Elizalde's focus on equitable access. Teacher quality investment as growth driver.
75231-disd.standardsFramework: Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills (TEKS). Must include TEKS codes. Also include English Language Proficiency Standards (ELPS) alignment. CS: TEKS Chapter 126. Science: TEKS Chapter 112. Math: TEKS Chapter 111.

engagementOptions.name: Intimate Cohort Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Hands-on PD with deep iteration per participant. Day 1: foundations and guided lesson design. Day 2: independent design, peer review, implementation planning. Best for grade-level teams, content-area cohorts, or single-school faculties.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Each participant leaves with 2–3 classroom-ready lessons they built themselves, plus shared facilitator notes.

engagementOptions.name: Building Faculty Workshop
engagementOptions.description: Whole-school PD with two facilitators running parallel break-out tracks (e.g., elementary / secondary, or content-area splits). Common opening and closing sessions; differentiated middle. Best for full-faculty rollouts where every teacher in the building participates.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Building-wide lesson library, cross-grade alignment notes, shared vocabulary and protocols.

engagementOptions.name: District-Wide Institute
engagementOptions.description: Multi-school institute with a full Freyja Labs facilitation team. Parallel grade-band tracks, daily plenary keynote and synthesis, and an admin strand running alongside. Built for district-level CS, AI literacy, or STEM integration rollouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: District lesson catalog, scope-and-sequence draft, leadership policy brief, year-one implementation roadmap.

engagementOptions.name: Leadership Orientation
engagementOptions.description: For leaders exploring AI literacy or CS PD. Hands-on demo using the same protocols teachers experience, policy landscape review, and a planning conversation about scope and rollout.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Policy brief, readiness assessment, engagement plan.

engagementOptions.name: Six-Week Virtual Coaching
engagementOptions.description: Post-workshop follow-up: implementation support, troubleshooting, lesson refinement. Scales to any cohort size with small-group breakouts.
engagementOptions.deliverables: Revised lessons, implementation log, student work portfolio.

tech.title: Equity in Data: Whose Voices Does AI Hear?
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Dallas ISD students in Dallas.
content_objectives: Collect real-time environmental data using micro:bit sensors deployed across campus
Analyze patterns in collected data using tables and graphs
Compare empirical measurements with model-generated predictions
ai_literacy_objectives: Evaluate AI-generated predictions against student-collected sensor data
Identify conditions under which AI predictions are reliable vs. misleading
Apply the Check the Machine protocol to decide when to trust, verify, or override AI
materials: AI analysis tool access (at landing page)

procedure.title: Collect
procedure.description: Teams of 3–5 deploy micro:bit sensors at locations around the school. Record temperature, light, and humidity at 5-minute intervals. Note direct observations — clouds, wind, shade patterns. Build a shared data table.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams input data into the AI analysis tool. AI generates predictions and pattern descriptions. Students compare: Does the AI match what we measured? Where does it agree? Where does it diverge? Document at least three points of agreement and three of disagreement.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Each team applies Check the Machine. Teams present: Where was AI accurate? Where did it miss? What did human observation catch that AI did not? Class develops shared guidelines for trusting AI with environmental data.

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses and instruments?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Given what we found, should we trust, adjust, or override the AI?

assessment.description: One-page Trust Report: where AI was accurate, where it failed, evidence-based recommendation.
assessment.criteria: Quality of evidence and reasoning, not whether student agreed with AI.

Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

differentiation.below_grade: Reduce to two artifacts in the packet (instead of three or four). Provide a graphic organizer with three columns: What the AI said / What I know / What I would tell someone who asked me. Sentence frames provided for the final argument.
differentiation.above_grade: Add a meta-question: students draft two or three rules for when the kind of AI tool in this lesson should and should not be used by adults in their lives. The rules are evidence-cited from the packet.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Encourage discussion in students' home language. The artifacts in the packet can be discussed in any language — the analysis transfers. Final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. Pair vocabulary cards if helpful: source, reasoning, reality, evidence.

exit_ticket.prompt: An AI tool gives someone you care about a recommendation. What three things should they check before they accept it?
look_for: At least one item references the source or input data the AI used
At least one item references the AI's reasoning or comparison with known facts
At least one item references checking with a person, lived experience, or independent source

"It depends" is allowed if followed by "specifically, on these things"

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student practiced something most adults haven't been formally taught: how to evaluate an AI-generated claim before accepting it.

In this lesson, students worked from printed artifacts — no screens — and applied a four-part protocol called Check the Machine: check the source, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself. They learned that the right answer to "should I trust this AI?" is almost always "let me check first."

At home, you can use the same protocol. The next time an AI assistant gives your family information, ask your student: "What would we need to check before we acted on this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Dallas ISD)

75231-disd.policyLandscape: CS = LOTE sub; TEA shortage area
75231-disd.districtNeeds: Texas districts have autonomy in how they approach CS and AI, and the CS teacher-shortage designation signals that demand is outpacing the supply of dedicated specialists. The more sustainable path is building computational thinking capacity across content areas — helping existing teachers develop pedagogical approaches that integrate computing into their practice. As AI tools become more present in classrooms, teachers also need a structured way to help students engage with those tools critically. The CRAFT framework provides that structure, and the Check the Machine protocol gives students a repeatable verification practice they can apply across subjects and tools.
75231-disd.contextNarrative: Dallas ISD is one of the more visible turnaround stories in U.S. urban education, with Superintendent Elizalde's leadership focused on equitable access and teacher quality as the engine of growth. That's the lens to read this work through: PD that meaningfully changes what teachers can do, not PD that just gets credentialed. Texas's CS teacher-shortage designation means the realistic path forward is computational thinking integration across content areas, not a hiring solution. CRAFT and Check the Machine are designed for that integration — frameworks teachers across math, science, and humanities can apply to their existing instruction and grow with over time.

tech.title: Consistent Systems: Reliable Data Across Classrooms
tech.overview: Students use micro:bit sensors to collect real-time environmental data, then compare their firsthand observations with AI-generated analysis. Through the Check the Machine protocol, students develop evidence-based criteria for when to trust, verify, or override AI outputs. Designed for Cypress-Fairbanks ISD students in Cypress.
content_objectives: Collect real-time environmental data using micro:bit sensors deployed across campus
Analyze patterns in collected data using tables and graphs
Compare empirical measurements with model-generated predictions
ai_literacy_objectives: Evaluate AI-generated predictions against student-collected sensor data
Identify conditions under which AI predictions are reliable vs. misleading
Apply the Check the Machine protocol to decide when to trust, verify, or override AI
materials: AI analysis tool access (at landing page)

procedure.title: Collect
procedure.description: Teams of 3–5 deploy micro:bit sensors at locations around the school. Record temperature, light, and humidity at 5-minute intervals. Note direct observations — clouds, wind, shade patterns. Build a shared data table.

procedure.title: Compare
procedure.description: Teams input data into the AI analysis tool. AI generates predictions and pattern descriptions. Students compare: Does the AI match what we measured? Where does it agree? Where does it diverge? Document at least three points of agreement and three of disagreement.

procedure.title: Evaluate
procedure.description: Each team applies Check the Machine. Teams present: Where was AI accurate? Where did it miss? What did human observation catch that AI did not? Class develops shared guidelines for trusting AI with environmental data.

check_the_machine.label: Check the source
check_the_machine.prompt: Where did the AI get its data? Is it the same data we collected?

check_the_machine.label: Check the reasoning
check_the_machine.prompt: How did the AI reach its conclusion? Can we follow the logic?

check_the_machine.label: Check against reality
check_the_machine.prompt: Does the output match what we observed with our own senses and instruments?

check_the_machine.label: Check your judgment
check_the_machine.prompt: Given what we found, should we trust, adjust, or override the AI?

assessment.description: One-page Trust Report: where AI was accurate, where it failed, evidence-based recommendation.
assessment.criteria: Quality of evidence and reasoning, not whether student agreed with AI.

extensions: Repeat data collection over multiple days
Design a new investigation using the same tools
Use CxEdHub lesson builder to create a variation for another subject

prior_knowledge: Read and create simple data tables and bar/line graphs
Distinguish between an observation (what we measured) and an inference (what we conclude)
Familiarity with one-step variable assignment in block-based or text-based code

facilitation_notes.focus: Don't standardize sensor placement across teams. Different microclimates make Phase 2 richer. Move between teams every 5 minutes; check that students are recording observations *and* numerical readings. The qualitative notes are the wedge they'll use to challenge AI in Phase 3.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams logging only numbers. Push them to write at least one observation per reading ("breeze picked up", "cloud passed over"). If the campus has visibly varied environments — shade vs. sun, paved vs. planted — push teams to spread out.

facilitation_notes.focus: Frame the AI tool as a teammate, not an authority. When the AI prediction is wrong, students often default to "we'll fix our data." Interrupt that — the goal is to surface where AI and ground-truth diverge, not to reconcile.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Teams that find zero divergence. Either they're smoothing data unconsciously, or the AI is generic enough to match anything. Have them pick a single 5-minute window and compare in extreme detail.

facilitation_notes.focus: The class trust guidelines are the deliverable. Push for specificity: not "AI is bad at humidity" but "AI underestimates humidity in conditions like ours when [specific local condition]." Local knowledge + data = the trust criteria.
facilitation_notes.watch_for: Generic statements ("AI is sometimes wrong"). Reject these gently — every guideline must reference a specific divergence the team observed.

misconceptions.misconception: "If the AI says it, it must be right — it has access to all the data."
misconceptions.response: Show the AI a deliberately wrong dataset and have students predict the (wrong) output. Reinforce: AI confidence ≠ AI correctness. The AI processes whatever input it receives, including noise and bias.

misconceptions.misconception: "Our sensor data is wrong because it doesn't match the AI."
misconceptions.response: Have students re-measure with a second device or different location. Direct measurement is the ground truth — divergence with AI is a signal worth investigating, not an error to "fix."

misconceptions.misconception: "The AI is broken if it gives a different answer to the same question twice."
misconceptions.response: This is a feature, not a bug. Use it to discuss probabilistic vs. deterministic systems. Two valid outputs can describe the same data — students should learn to ask "what stayed the same?"

differentiation.below_grade: Pre-load the data table with column headers and one example row. Pair with a peer for the AI comparison phase. Provide a sentence frame for the evaluate phase: "We trust the AI when ___ because ___. We don't trust it when ___ because ___."
differentiation.above_grade: Add a fourth phase: students design a follow-up experiment that would resolve a specific AI-vs-observation disagreement they found. They write the protocol; they don't need to execute it in class.
differentiation.multilingual_learners: Provide vocabulary cards in advance: sensor, prediction, observation, evidence, divergence. Allow team discussion in students' home language; final class artifacts can be authored bilingually. The Check the Machine protocol works in any language — emphasize that direct measurement and lived knowledge are the most authoritative inputs.

exit_ticket.prompt: Describe one moment today when your direct measurement told you something the AI missed. What did you measure, and what should the AI have done differently?
look_for: Specific reference to a measurement (number + unit + location)
Specific reference to what the AI output said
A concrete claim about what the AI should have changed (input, comparison, caveat)

slide_cues.title: What we are doing today
talking_points: Collect real data on our campus
Compare it with what an AI predicts
Decide together when AI is worth trusting

slide_cues.title: The Check the Machine protocol
talking_points: Source · Reasoning · Reality · Yourself
You don't need to know how the AI works to check its work
A protocol, not a checklist — adapt to the situation

slide_cues.title: Phase 1 — Collect
talking_points: Teams of 3 deploy sensors at chosen spots
Record at intervals; vary your locations
Log a written observation with each reading

slide_cues.title: Phase 2 — Compare
talking_points: Input your data into the AI tool
Find at least 3 places it agrees, 3 places it diverges
Don't correct your data to match the AI

slide_cues.title: Phase 3 — Evaluate
talking_points: Apply Check the Machine to your divergences
Present one trust guideline based on what you found
Class builds the shared trust framework together

slide_cues.title: Exit ticket
talking_points: One moment when your measurement beat the AI
Be specific — number, location, what AI missed

teacher_materials.family_letter:
Dear families,

This week your student is learning a skill that will matter for the rest of their lives: how to decide when to trust an AI system.

In this lesson, students used real sensors to measure conditions around our school and compared what they measured with what an AI predicted. The point is not that AI is bad — the point is that AI works best when paired with someone who knows the real situation. Your student is learning to be that someone.

We call the protocol Check the Machine. It has four steps: check the source the AI used, check the reasoning, check the result against reality, and check yourself for what you might have missed. You can use this with your student at home — every time an AI assistant gives you an answer, ask: "How would we check this?"

Questions? hello@freyjalabs.com
— Freyja Labs (working with Cypress-Fairbanks ISD)
